results.possibleIdentity: Richard Schulman is a prominent New York-based photographer and writer whose career began in the late 1960s or early 1970s. He is best known for his architectural photography and his portraits of iconic 20th-century artists and architects, including Philip Johnson, Andy Warhol, Jean-Michel Basquiat, John Cage, and Merce Cunningham. His work is closely associated with the New York avant-garde circle that founded Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); his archives and photographs are documented in the research files of the Robert Rauschenberg Foundation and the Calvin Tomkins Papers at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), which chronicle the history of E.A.T. Schulman often frames his own creative process through the lens of science and mathematics, describing photography as an intersection of 'heart, soul, and numbers' and referencing 'experiments' with light and technology. He has published several books, including 'Portraits of the New Architecture' and 'Portraits of the New Architecture 2', and has taught at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) and Parsons School of Design.

Richard Schulman (1947–2013), also known by the pseudonym Citizen Kafka, was an artist, collector, folk musician, and radio personality active in the New York City counterculture and avant-garde scene starting in the late 1960s. He was a resident of 537 Broadway in SoHo, a building that served as a hub for the Fluxus movement and artist cooperatives organized by George Maciunas. As Citizen Kafka, he hosted influential programs on WBAI radio, such as 'The Wretched Refuse String Band' and 'The Citizen Kafka Show,' which frequently featured experimental music and performances. His activities as an 'artist and collector' within the SoHo community overlapped with the era and social circles of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). His life and work as a performance artist and documenter of the scene are archived in collections such as the 'High Performance' magazine records at the Getty Research Institute.
